notes.anchor: We spoke before the pontiffs on the day before the Kalends of October
notes.note: This is the speech De Domo Sua, delivered on 29 September 57 BC to the College of Pontiffs. The board had to decide whether Clodius's consecration of Cicero's house-site barred its restitution.

notes.anchor: he who said he had dedicated
notes.note: The pontiffs' ruling turned on a technicality: a valid dedication required that the man performing it be named to the task by a vote of the people. Since Clodius had no such authorisation, the consecration could be lifted.

notes.anchor: a public meeting which Appius had given him
notes.note: A contio, an informal public assembly that any magistrate could convene to address the people without a vote. Appius Claudius Pulcher, Clodius's elder brother and a praetor in 57 BC, summoned this one so Clodius could spin the pontiffs' verdict.

A colonnade built by Q. Lutatius Catulus (cos. 78 BC) that adjoined Cicero's house; Clodius had demolished it to extend his own grounds. Its restitution by public contract was bound up with Cicero's own.
M. Terentius Varro Lucullus (consul 73 BC), a senior pontiff and brother of the great Lucullus. He drew the crucial distinction: the priests judged the religious question, the Senate the legal one.

Sex. Atilius Serranus, a tribune of 57 BC, used the tribunician veto (intercessio) to block the Senate's decree. A single tribune could thus halt senatorial business, the chief obstructive weapon of the period.

notes.anchor: throwing off his toga, he cast himself at his son-in-law's feet
notes.note: A theatrical gesture of supplication: Cornicinus dramatised entreaty by stripping to his tunic and falling at Serranus's feet to win him a delay. Cicero scorns it as a stale piece of stagecraft ('his old play').

notes.anchor: they remembered the Kalends of January
notes.note: A bitter memory: when a previous obstructionist had been granted an overnight adjournment, the delay had let opponents wreck the business by the next day's session. The senators feared a repeat.

notes.anchor: valued the upper part of the house at two million sesterces
notes.note: The consuls, on their advisory council, assessed compensation for Cicero's losses: 2,000,000 sesterces for the house's superstructure, 500,000 for the Tusculan villa, 250,000 for the Formian. Cicero thought the country-house figures meanly low.

A legatus was a senior deputy appointed to a commander's staff; the post let a senator travel on public business while keeping his standing. Cicero took a free-floating legateship under Pompey's grain command so as to keep his options open.

A legatio votiva (or libera) was a nominal commission allowing a senator to travel abroad on the pretext of fulfilling a vow at temples, with the privileges of an envoy but no real duties. Cicero declines it to stay visible at Rome.
